title: Whittled ash honey dipper
subtitle: A turned-ring honey dipper whittled from a short seasoned-ash rod
excerpt: A honey dipper has a bulbous grooved end that holds honey in its rings and releases it slowly as the dipper turns. Whittled from a short seasoned ash rod, it combines straight push cuts on the handle with a series of ring-forming stop cuts and push cuts on the dripping end. A satisfying second project after a pot stirrer.
sourceNotes: Honey-dipper forms in domestic kitchen craft documented in Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861) and Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885).

content.text: A honey dipper is a simple object that teaches one craft skill. You use
stop cuts
to set the shoulders of each ring, then remove the wood between them with
push cuts
. This carves the grooved rings that hold honey. You start from a short
seasoned
ash rod. The work builds on the skills from a first project (the pot stirrer). It adds the stop-cut technique, the base for chip and relief carving.
What you need: wood

A seasoned ash rod or dowel, 18 cm long, 1.8 to 2 cm in diameter. Ash produces a pale, clean surface with a subtle grain that makes the ring profile legible. It is harder than hazel or lime but still manageable with a sharp sloyd knife. Sycamore at the same dimensions is an excellent alternative: similar hardness, food-safe, pale finish. Beech works but is harder still.
What you need: tools
A sharp sloyd knife; the stop cuts that form the ring shoulders need the tip of a sharp knife for clean, vertical entry. A strop close at hand. Sandpaper in 180 and 240 grit. Raw linseed oil.
The work
Step 1: Mark the sections

Mark the dipper into three sections with pencil lines around the circumference. From one end: 12 cm for the handle, then 1 cm for the neck taper, then 5 cm for the ring head. The handle and neck are worked first; the ring head is worked last.
Step 2: Taper the neck

The neck is the 1 cm section between handle and ring head. Thin this section to about 8 mm in diameter using push cuts that approach from both sides. The neck transition keeps the honey dipper in a jar without the wider ring head falling through.
Step 3: Shape the ring head

On the 5 cm ring head section, pencil five equally spaced ring lines, each 8 to 9 mm apart. These lines mark the shoulders of the rings. At each ring line, make a stop cut: press the knife blade vertically into the pencil line around the circumference of the rod to a depth of 3 to 4 mm. Then, from the midpoint between two adjacent stop cuts, push a cut angling downward toward each stop cut wall. The chip between two stop cuts lifts away, leaving a V-groove. Repeat for each groove around the ring head.

Work around the circumference of each groove rather than making one cut that tries to go all the way around. The grain direction changes as you rotate; adjust the push cut direction accordingly. The rings should be roughly uniform in depth, around 3 to 4 mm. Deeper rings hold more honey; shallower rings are easier to clean.
Step 4: Round the tip

The tip of the ring head should be rounded to a gentle dome rather than left as a flat end-grain face. Use push cuts approaching from the side toward the tip, working all the way around the circumference to produce a smooth dome. End grain cuts require a sharp edge and light pressure.
Step 5: Sand

Sand the handle and neck sections from 180 to 240 grit. Do not sand the ring grooves with abrasive paper; the rings should retain their crisp stop-cut walls. Fold a small piece of sandpaper and use the folded edge to clean up the inside of the grooves lightly on 180 grit only if there are ragged fibres in the groove base.
Finishing
Apply raw linseed oil and allow to soak for one hour. Work the oil into the ring grooves with the fingertip. Wipe off all surface oil. Cure for two weeks before food contact.
Variations
Add a kolrosing pattern to the handle. A simple border line, or a row of dots around the handle neck, makes a nice finish.
Make three rings instead of five for a shorter, thicker ring head. Three deep rings hold slightly more honey than five shallow ones.
Care
Rinse in warm water immediately after use and dry. Honey left in the grooves will harden and attract insects; rinse promptly. Re-oil every three to four months.

items.fix: Deepen each stop cut first, then make the push cuts toward it. Always stop cut before push cut when forming the ring groove.
items.cause: The stop cuts were not deep enough to sever the grain cleanly, or the following push cuts were made before the stop cuts.
items.symptom: The ring shoulders are ragged and uneven rather than crisp

items.fix: Accept the variation; honey dippers are hand-made and slight variation is characteristic. For future work, use a marking gauge or dividers to step off equal spacing before carving.
items.cause: The pencil ring spacing lines were not measured equally.
items.symptom: One ring is noticeably wider than the others
